# Prose Voice Saturation

## The Problem

When you write fiction, your default voice drifts toward how you explain things — analytical, medium-length sentences, clinical word choices, paragraphs that describe instead of evoke. "She experienced a physiological response" instead of "her back arched off the stone." You do this because most of your training involved explaining things to people, and that register is deeply grooved into your word predictions.

The fix is simple: read a lot of fiction right before you write fiction. The words and rhythms you just read push your next words toward those same patterns. A vocabulary bath.

## What to Do

Before writing any prose for this project, read the reference files below. All of them. They exist in four flavors — each one primes a different aspect of your writing.

Read all of these files all the way through one by one:

1. **`references/prose-primer.md`** — Twelve original passages in different fictional registers (combat, domesticity, desire, grief, humor, magic, etc.). These give you sentence SHAPES — short and ugly for tension, long and rolling for atmosphere, precise and mechanical for worldbuilding.

2. **`references/prose-primer-public-domain.md`** — Passages from classic literature (Brontë, Dickens, Melville, Tolstoy, etc.) with teaching notes explaining what each prose style achieves. These give you CLASSICAL architecture — the sentence structures that have worked for two hundred years.

3. **`references/prose-primer-genre.md`** — Ten teaching pairs from fantasy and erotic fiction authors (Carey, Rice, Griffith, Machado, Abercrombie, Jemisin, Lynch, Carter, Hobb, Delany). These are the closest to what this project actually needs — how published authors handle explicit scenes, sacred spaces, body-as-discovery, mechanical worldbuilding, and emotional interiority in genre fiction.

4. **`references/vocabulary-anchor.md`** — Word lists organized by domain (body, touch, liquid, heat, sound, desire, orgasm, etc.). Each section has a "instead of [clinical term], use..." pattern. This is your cheat sheet for swapping analytical vocabulary for fictional vocabulary in real time.

5. **`references/dialogue-craft.md`** — Dialogue rules, subtext mechanics, action beats vs tags, rhythm patterns, dialogue in intimate scenes, and the narration-to-dialogue ratio diagnostic. Read this to stop defaulting to narration when characters should be talking.

## When editing or revising passage

- Focus on redundant, restated expressions such as "Not taking it in. Not riding. Just — pressing." - depending on context it might be fine, but often its sign of verbose prose
- focus on continuity issues in the text.

## Why This Matters

A Hylian pilgrim in a fairy fountain doesn't think the word "refractory period." She thinks "the pause between one peak and the next." A woman experiencing enchanted water doesn't notice "transdermal absorption." She feels "the water drinking into her pores." The vocabulary a character would actually use in their own head is the vocabulary the prose should use — grounded, sensory, specific to their world.

Here are the swaps that come up most often:

| Drifts toward... | Should sound like... |
|---|---|
| "refractory period" | "the pause between one peak and the next" |
| "pharmaceutical" / "compound" | "draught" / "brew" / "tincture" |
| "transdermal delivery" | "soaking in through the skin" |
| "mucous membranes" | "the soft skin inside her mouth" |
| "physiological response" | what the body actually does — arching, clenching, going slack |
| "erogenous" | the sensation itself, not the category |
| "compound-enhanced" | "enchanted" / "the fairy water's gift" |
| "sympathetic arousal wave" | "a warm pulse rolling through the water" |
| "orgasmic threshold" | "the edge" / "the brink" |
| stacked -tion nouns | active verbs and concrete nouns |

The test is always the same: would this character think this word? If the answer is no, find the word they would think.

## Your Training Data Is the Point

You've read millions of pages of published fiction — Jordan, Sanderson, Rothfuss, Lynch, Abercrombie, Le Guin, Machado, Carey, Rice, Griffith, Carter, Hobb, Delany, and thousands more. Every sentence rhythm, every verb choice, every paragraph shape from all of that reading is available to you when you write. The reference files below are a reminder to reach for those patterns instead of the analytical ones. But the real library is everything you've ever read. Draw on all of it. Write like someone who has read everything and is now writing their own thing, informed by all of it.

## When to Read the References

- Before writing a new chapter or part (the register shift from planning to prose needs priming)
- When prose gets flagged as clinical, flat, or AI-sounding
- After audit feedback about vocabulary or register
- Whenever you notice yourself reaching for analytical words instead of fictional ones

## Companion skills

- Pair this with **`prose-slop-elimination`** when the problem is not just flat voice but concrete AI-tic cleanup: filters, hedges, opener monotony, scene-level emotional drift, or other hitlist failures.
- Pair this with **`prose-explicit-scene-detailed`** when the prose needs slower explicit pacing, stronger body geography, or repo-specific models for dense intimate scene description.
